skill.name: seed-data-generator
skill.description: Generate realistic seed data and seeding scripts for development databases, tests, and product demos.
skill.summary: Create realistic database seed data while preserving schema relationships and common test scenarios.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: The static external command, path traversal, sensitive key, and system reconnaissance findings are largely false positives from Markdown code fences, shell usage examples, relative imports, dictionary key iteration, and SQL examples. The skill is not malicious, but it includes destructive database clearing examples and insecure placeholder password data, so it should publish with a development-only warning.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Database Seeding Examples
medium_findings.description: The Prisma and Django examples clear existing records before inserting seed data, and the skill also shows commands to run seed scripts. This is legitimate for development, but dangerous if users run it against production or shared staging databases.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples plainly delete table contents and provide commands that execute seed routines. The context is development seeding, so the risk is misuse rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Insecure Placeholder Password Data
medium_findings.description: Several examples generate password_hash values with random strings or SHA-256 faker output. These are acceptable test placeholders, but they could create unsafe habits if copied into authentication code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is explicitly test-data generation, and one line notes that real applications should use bcrypt. The pattern still deserves a warning because the field names represent password hashes.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The scanner identified many fenced Markdown code blocks as Ruby backtick execution. Review found documentation examples and a few explicit seed commands, with no evidence that the skill itself executes arbitrary shell input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown fences, SQL examples, or documented CLI commands. No dynamic command construction or hidden execution path was found in the single skill file.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Findings Are Relative Imports
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections point to TypeScript import paths for local entity modules. They do not read user-controlled paths or traverse the filesystem at runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are static import statements inside a TypeORM example. There is no file read, file write, or user-controlled path handling at those locations.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Key Finding Is Dictionary Key Iteration
low_findings.description: The sensitive certificate or key-file detection points to a Python call that iterates over dictionary keys for organization size weights. No certificate, private key, or credential file is present at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged line uses ORG_SIZES.keys() inside a data generation example. It does not reference key files, private keys, or filesystem paths.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are SQL Variables
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance detections are inside PostgreSQL seed functions and refer to local variables, return declarations, selected IDs, or updates. They do not inspect the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are database seed function syntax and local SQL statements. No host commands, environment probing, or machine inventory collection appears there.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Review did not find instructions that claim special authority, ask the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, or attempt to override security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction section describes normal seed-data generation behavior. No suspicious override language or evaluator manipulation was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bulk Deletion Before Seeding
dangerous_patterns.description: Example seeders delete existing rows before inserting new records. Users need environment checks and backups before running generated seed scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The delete calls are direct and easy to verify. The danger depends on the database target rather than hidden malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Seed Commands That Mutate Databases
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows commands that run seed routines against the configured database. These commands can change or delete data if environment settings are wrong.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are legitimate framework commands, but they intentionally mutate a database. Risk is clear when command environment is misconfigured.

content.value_statement: Teams need useful test data that matches real schema relationships. This skill helps create SQL and ORM seeders for development, testing, and demos.
actual_capabilities: Analyzes database tables, relationships, constraints, and common data patterns.
Generates SQL insert examples and procedural PostgreSQL seed functions.
Creates JavaScript and TypeScript seeders for Prisma and TypeORM workflows.
Creates Python seeding examples for Django management commands and Factory Boy.
Suggests realistic e-commerce and SaaS data scenarios with related records.
Recommends seed ordering, reproducible random seeds, and cleanup practices.
limitations: It does not inspect a live database unless the user provides schema details.
Generated scripts must be reviewed before running against any shared database.
It uses fake data and should not generate real personal information.
It does not replace migration tooling or database backup procedures.

use_cases.title: Build Development Fixtures
use_cases.description: Create realistic users, products, orders, and related records for local application development.

use_cases.title: Prepare Demo Environments
use_cases.description: Populate staging demos with believable activity, status mixes, and historical timestamps.

use_cases.title: Expand Test Coverage
use_cases.description: Generate edge cases and relationship-heavy records for integration and acceptance tests.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Seed Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate seed data for my users and products tables. Include realistic names, emails, prices, and timestamps.

prompt_templates.title: Match My Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this schema to create seed data with valid foreign keys and insert order. Explain any assumptions before writing the seed plan.

prompt_templates.title: Generate ORM Seeder
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Prisma seeder for users, categories, products, orders, and order items. Include development-only safety checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design Scenario Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Design realistic SaaS seed scenarios for free, team, and enterprise accounts. Include edge cases, relationship rules, and reproducible seed settings.

output_examples.input: A request for e-commerce seed data with users, products, orders, and reviews.
output_examples.output: A seeding plan with parent-child ordering, realistic quantity targets, status distributions, and review coverage rules.

output_examples.input: A Prisma project that needs repeatable local development data.
output_examples.output: A Prisma-oriented seeder outline with table cleanup order, generated records, related order items, and a reproducible random seed.

output_examples.input: A SaaS schema with organizations, users, projects, and roles.
output_examples.output: A multi-tenant data scenario with organization size bands, role distribution, project ownership, and realistic creation dates.

best_practices: Run generated seeders only against local or approved staging databases.
Review delete, truncate, and cleanup steps before executing any seed command.
Use fixed random seeds when tests require repeatable records.
anti_patterns: Running generated seed scripts against production databases.
Using fake password hashes or placeholder secrets in real authentication flows.
Generating unrelated records without preserving foreign key relationships.

faq.question: Can this skill generate data for any database?
faq.answer: It can adapt to common SQL databases and ORM patterns when you provide schema details.

faq.question: Does it connect to my database automatically?
faq.answer: No. It produces guidance and seed script examples based on the schema or requirements you provide.

faq.question: Is the generated data safe for production?
faq.answer: No. Treat generated data and cleanup scripts as development or staging tools only.

faq.question: Can it preserve foreign key relationships?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill focuses on parent-child ordering and relationship-aware record generation.

faq.question: Can I request edge cases?
faq.answer: Yes. Ask for specific edge cases such as inactive users, cancelled orders, empty inventory, or large tenants.

faq.question: Does it support reproducible data?
faq.answer: Yes. It can suggest fixed random seeds so tests receive consistent generated records.
